text.title: Recruitment Funnel Stages and Benchmarks
text.summary: Six-stage hiring funnel with benchmark conversion rates and optimization levers per stage.

rule.statement: Track conversion rate at every funnel stage; a stage without a metric cannot be optimized.
rule.rationale: Funnel leakage is invisible without conversion data. ATS and career-page analytics provide the raw counts; the ratio reveals the bottleneck.

section.title: Stage 1: Awareness

rule.statement: Measure career page views and apply-click rate as the Awareness-to-Application indicator.
rule.rationale: No benchmark exists for awareness volume alone; what matters is whether visitors convert to applicants.

section.p: Optimization levers: career page SEO, employer brand content, job board and aggregator distribution, employee referral program, social media presence.

section.title: Stage 2: Application

rule.statement: Application conversion benchmark is 8-12%; below 8% signals JD friction or application UX problems.
rule.rationale: A long application form, missing mobile support, or unclear JD causes candidates to abandon before submitting.

section.p: Optimization levers: simplify application to under 5 minutes, remove non-essential fields, ensure mobile-friendly form, send auto-response on submit.

section.title: Stage 3: Screening

rule.statement: Screen-to-interview conversion benchmark is 20-30%; outside this range signals sourcing quality or criteria clarity problems.
rule.rationale: Below 20% means sourcing is generating noise; above 30% may mean screening criteria are too loose.

section.p: Optimization levers: define clear screening criteria before posting, automate initial resume pass, schedule phone screen within 1 week, send timely rejections.

section.title: Stage 4: Interview

rule.statement: Interview-to-offer conversion benchmark is 15-25%; deviation indicates process efficiency or evaluation consistency issues.
rule.rationale: Too low means the interview process is filtering beyond the intended bar or taking too long. Too high may indicate insufficient rigor.

section.p: Optimization levers: structured interview process, calibrated interviewers, scheduling within 1 week, positive candidate experience, timely feedback.

section.title: Stage 5: Offer

rule.statement: Offer acceptance benchmark is 85-95%; below 85% requires root-cause analysis of declined reasons before adjusting compensation.
rule.rationale: Declines split across compensation, competing offers, and role/location mismatches. Each has a different fix; conflating them wastes budget.

section.p: Optimization levers: issue offer within 48 hours of decision, make a personal hiring-manager call, prepare for negotiation with defined limits, preempt competing offers with verbal commitments.

rule.statement: Track 90-day retention as the funnel's terminal metric; target 90%+.
rule.rationale: A hire who leaves in 90 days represents the full recruiting cost with zero return. Onboarding quality is a recruiting metric, not only an HR metric.

section.title: Key Metrics

rule.statement: Report time-to-fill, cost-per-hire, and quality-of-hire alongside conversion rates; volume metrics alone mask quality degradation.
rule.rationale: Optimizing application volume without tracking quality-of-hire floods the funnel with mismatched candidates and inflates downstream costs.

section.p: Metric targets: time-to-fill under 45 days; cost-per-hire under $4,000; offer acceptance above 85%; quality-of-hire (meeting expectations at 1 year) above 80%.
